text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE


Clinic Date: 25 April 2015

Dr Amrita Patel
Grove Park Health Centre

123 Grove Park Road
London SE5 8QX
Dear Dr Amrita Patel
Sophie Turner, DOB: 14-03-2004, Hospital No: H739201 NHS No. 8429105673
Flat 12, 28 Beech Grove, London SE15 3QD 

Diagnoses/summary: Transition from paediatric to adult neurology care. Seizure history since childhood; a consistent adult phenotype has not been clearly established to date. No prior prolonged admissions for status reported. Collateral information occasionally provided by parent when attending.

Previous investigations (per paediatric records): Routine EEGs reported as non-diagnostic; prior MRI reportedly normal in childhood (images not currently available on our PACS).

Current concerns and history: I reviewed Sophie Turner in the adult neurology transition clinic today. She attended alone; her mother sometimes accompanies to appointments and offers collateral history, but was not present today. She reports that her day-to-day functioning is generally stable, in work part-time, and not currently driving. She keeps a simple paper seizure diary.

Seizure pattern: She may remain seizure-free for up to four month, but then will experience clusters of 2 to 4 seizures in a single day. She describes a brief warning of feeling "detached" followed by loss of awareness for under 2 minutes with quick recovery, rarely with tongue-bite; no clear convulsions witnessed in the last year per her account. No consistent circadian trigger. She notes stress and sleep loss may precede cluster days.

Medication history: She is currently not on regular antiseizure medication; she elected to stop prior adolescent therapy several years ago due to perceived low frequency and side effects (sedation and low mood). She recalls previous trials in childhood but cannot recall drug names or doses. No known drug allergies.

Comorbidities/other: Migraine without aura approximately once per month, managed with over-the-counter analgesia. Normal birth and development reported. No family history of seizures known. Menstrual cycles regular; no current pregnancy plans.

Examination today: General and neurological examinations were unremarkable. BP 112/70, HR 72, weight 63 kg. No objective cognitive concerns on brief screening.

Plan:
- Transition consolidation: requested full paediatric records including EEG/MRI reports and medication history; will import into adult record when received.
- Safety: provided written seizure first-aid advice and discussed avoidance of sleep deprivation; advised on bath and height safety. Driving guidance reiterated as per UK DVLA regulations given ongoing events.
- Monitoring: continue seizure diary with dates and, on any cluster day, number of events and approximate times.
- Investigations: arrange adult routine EEG and request prior MRI images; consider updated MRI if historical images cannot be retrieved.
- Follow-up: review in 4 months in the adult epilepsy clinic or sooner if clusters increase in frequency or features change. Parent welcome to attend to provide collateral history if available.

Thank you for your ongoing care and for forwarding any additional information from primary care records that might assist in clarifying the historical treatment and investigation details.

Yours sincerely

attributes.Label: 1 cluster per 4 month, 2 to 4 per cluster

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly describes the seizure pattern: she may be seizure-free for up to four months, then experiences clusters of 2 to 4 seizures in a single day. This implies a cluster approximately every four months, with 2 to 4 seizures per cluster. There is no more frequent alternative frequency stated. Not seizure-free for ≥6 months, so not labeled as seizure-free. Therefore, the normalized label should reflect cluster frequency and number per cluster.
seizure_frequency_number: 1 cluster per 4 month, 2 to 4 per cluster
